transcript:
Here's what's happening: you're watching two crowds of short curved marks—warm orange-and-amber ones, cool blue-grey ones—drift toward each other from opposite edges, tangling in the middle, while a dark fibrous mass seems to sweep the center clear.

The thing is, those two crowds aren't actually two colors mixing — look at the class called "What Passes Through." Each mark carries a variable called heritage, a number from zero to one that quietly shifts every time it gets close to a mark from the other side, in a function literally named "receive what passes through." The color function even has a comment: "not blending — interpenetration," routing hues through murky grey-olive territory instead of letting orange and blue make a clean secondary color. So marks that started pure clayfire or pure inkwell end up chromatically undecidable — the code tracks exactly how confused each one has become, in a variable called interpenetrations, and marks with too many get a tremor, an instability, because — the comment says — "the mark no longer knows what it is."

So the dark clearing in the middle isn't erasure. It's every mark forgetting which side it came from at once.
